title: AML Agents Briefing — Thursday, 25 June 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing for Wednesday the twenty-fifth of June 2026. I'm Alex, joined as always by Jordan. This is your audio overview of the most material AML and financial crime developments across the Asia-Pacific region today. Full detail, the obligation register, and horizon scanning are all at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Big day, Alex. We've got agentic AI moving into the financial crime infrastructure layer, two significant stablecoin developments with direct APAC regulatory read-across, and some important obligations and enforcement signals that MLROs need to act on this week. Let's get into it.

ALEX: Starting with the story that caught my attention this morning. SBI Holdings has co-led a five point six million dollar pre-Series A round in Pints AI, an agentic AI platform targeting financial services. Now, SBI is not a passive observer here. This is a tier-one Asian financial group putting capital directly into the infrastructure layer that sits beneath AI-assisted compliance and operations.

JORDAN: And the distinction matters, doesn't it? There's a difference between a bank buying a software licence from a vendor and a bank investing in the platform that other vendors will be built on top of.

ALEX: Exactly. SBI is effectively making a bet on the orchestration layer itself. For MLROs and heads of financial crime technology across the region, this signals that the competitive dynamic around AI in compliance is shifting. The question is no longer whether to adopt AI-assisted transaction monitoring or KYC automation. It's who controls the underlying infrastructure.

JORDAN: So the action item here is straightforward. If you're the head of financial crime compliance at a regional bank or a large securities firm, take this to your CTO or your chief data officer before the end of the week. Ask specifically whether your current AI vendors are building on top of agentic orchestration layers, and whether your own group has a view on that infrastructure dependency. It's a governance question as much as a technology question, and it belongs in your next risk committee pack.

ALEX: Moving on to stablecoins, and there are two developments here worth pairing together. Ripple's RLUSD stablecoin has received regulatory approval to operate in Japan. This is one of the first cases of a US-headquartered issuer clearing Japan's revised stablecoin licensing framework, which came into force following the 2022 amendments to Japan's Payment Services Act.

JORDAN: Japan's framework is genuinely one of the more structured stablecoin regimes in the region right now. Approval there isn't a formality. Ripple has had to demonstrate reserve adequacy, redemption mechanisms, and AML controls that satisfy the Financial Services Agency. That raises the bar for what other issuers in APAC will need to show.

ALEX: And on the same day, US-based Stablecore has launched a stablecoin and digital asset programme aimed specifically at credit unions in the United States. Now, credit unions have historically operated under lighter supervisory frameworks than commercial banks. Integrating virtual assets into that channel is worth watching closely.

JORDAN: The read-across for APAC compliance officers is this. If your institution is a correspondent bank, a payment processor, or a virtual asset service provider with any exposure to US credit union counterparties, you should be reviewing your counterparty due diligence framework now. Credit unions gaining stablecoin access expands your VASP exposure universe in ways your existing onboarding criteria may not have anticipated. Add it to your next thematic review scope.

ALEX: Before we close on horizon scanning, there are two enforcement and obligation signals that listeners should have on their radar this week. AUSTRAC has announced a forty-seven million Australian dollar civil penalty against a major registered digital currency exchange for systemic failures in transaction monitoring, mule-account detection, and suspicious matter report timeliness. This caps a twelve-month investigation.

JORDAN: The lesson from that enforcement for any AUSTRAC-regulated digital currency exchange, or any entity sitting inside tranche two of the AML/CTF Act reform programme, is specific. Regulators are not treating mule-account detection as a nice-to-have. It is now a core expectation alongside transaction monitoring. If you haven't already convened your transaction monitoring team to review your mule-account detection logic and your SMR timeliness metrics, do that this week.

ALEX: And on obligations, Indonesian reporting entities should note that the annual independent review of the AML and CFT programme covering the 2024 calendar year period is due to the Risk Oversight Committee and the Board of Commissioners under POJK 12 of 2017. If your institution hasn't completed that review and presented findings to the Dewan Komisaris, that needs to happen now. The deadline is the thirtieth of June.

JORDAN: Hong Kong-regulated entities, the HKMA self-assessment return covering the 2024 reporting period is due by the thirtieth of September, but the internal audit validation requirement means you should be commissioning that work now rather than in August. Pull the HKMA-prescribed Excel template, confirm your internal audit resource is scoped in, and don't leave this to the summer holiday period.

ALEX: So what are the two or three things listeners should do coming out of today's briefing?

JORDAN: First, if you have any VASP or stablecoin counterparty exposure, update your due diligence framework to reflect the expanding credit union channel in the United States. Second, if you're AUSTRAC-regulated, run a specific review of your mule-account detection rules and your SMR timeliness data before your next compliance committee meeting. Third, Indonesian entities, the thirtieth of June is five days away. Confirm your annual AML programme review is complete and presented to the board.

JORDAN: Full detail on all of today's stories, the complete obligation register, and our horizon scanning feed are at amlagents dot streamlit dot app. Everything you need to brief your team or prepare for your next risk committee is there.

ALEX: That's the briefing for the twenty-fifth of June. We'll be back tomorrow. Stay sharp.
